ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable and surprising reframing: that single-layer, random data-independent filters (with multi-scale and nonlinearities) can rival deep VGG-based texture synthesis, challenging the assumed necessity of depth and supervised pretraining. This is clearly differentiated from Gatys et al. and Portilla & Simoncelli in methodology and claims, and the use of Gram matrices + L-BFGS-B on single-layer multi-scale representations is a non-trivial, conceptually informative extension rather than a mere engineering tweak. From a theoretical-expert perspective the idea would likely surprise and inform, though it builds on existing Gram-matrix formulations rather than introducing an entirely new mathematical framework, so it is notable but not radical. | B(4): This paper presents a notable and surprising perspective: that single-layer, even random, convolutional feature statistics (Gram matrices) plus multi-scale filters and nonlinearities suffice to synthesize high-quality textures comparable to VGG-based multi-layer methods. The formulation is not an entirely new problem (texture synthesis is well-studied) but clearly differentiates itself from Gatys et al. by removing depth and pretraining, and by empirically demonstrating the importance of scale and nonlinearity rather than supervised depth. The techniques are a non-trivial, empirically grounded simplification that would inform practitioners and challenge assumptions about necessary complexity, though it builds on existing Gram-matrix optimization frameworks rather than introducing radically new algorithms.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and surprising reframing: that single-layer, random data-independent filters (with multi-scale and nonlinearities) can rival deep VGG-based texture synthesis, challenging the assumed necessity of depth and supervised pretraining. This is clearly differentiated from Gatys et al. and Portilla & Simoncelli in methodology and claims, and the use of Gram matrices + L-BFGS-B on single-layer multi-scale representations is a non-trivial, conceptually informative extension rather than a mere engineering tweak. From a theoretical-expert perspective the idea would likely surprise and inform, though it builds on existing Gram-matrix formulations rather than introducing an entirely new mathematical framework, so it is notable but not radical.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper presents a notable and surprising perspective: that single-layer, even random, convolutional feature statistics (Gram matrices) plus multi-scale filters and nonlinearities suffice to synthesize high-quality textures comparable to VGG-based multi-layer methods. The formulation is not an entirely new problem (texture synthesis is well-studied) but clearly differentiates itself from Gatys et al. by removing depth and pretraining, and by empirically demonstrating the importance of scale and nonlinearity rather than supervised depth. The techniques are a non-trivial, empirically grounded simplification that would inform practitioners and challenge assumptions about necessary complexity, though it builds on existing Gram-matrix optimization frameworks rather than introducing radically new algorithms.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (single-layer random-filter Gram models vs VGG and Portilla & Simoncelli), with methods, experiments and conclusions following logically. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported; figures and examples are informative and writing quality is described as good. Minor presentation weaknesses include some ambiguity around evaluation metrics (the VGG-based loss is acknowledged as an imperfect perceptual proxy) and the discussion of computational inefficiency for large multi-scale filters could be slightly clearer for reproducibility and comparison. | B(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early and clearly, describing the single-layer Gram-matrix approach and multi-scale variants. Notation and methodological details (including hyperparameters and optimization via L-BFGS-B) are presented consistently, and figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate comparisons to VGG-based and Portilla & Simoncelli baselines. Writing quality is good with minimal ambiguity, though the manuscript could more clearly flag practical limitations (computational inefficiency of large filters, lack of code release) and the imperfect nature of the VGG-based evaluation metric.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (single-layer random-filter Gram models vs VGG and Portilla & Simoncelli), with methods, experiments and conclusions following logically. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported; figures and examples are informative and writing quality is described as good. Minor presentation weaknesses include some ambiguity around evaluation metrics (the VGG-based loss is acknowledged as an imperfect perceptual proxy) and the discussion of computational inefficiency for large multi-scale filters could be slightly clearer for reproducibility and comparison.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early and clearly, describing the single-layer Gram-matrix approach and multi-scale variants. Notation and methodological details (including hyperparameters and optimization via L-BFGS-B) are presented consistently, and figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate comparisons to VGG-based and Portilla & Simoncelli baselines. Writing quality is good with minimal ambiguity, though the manuscript could more clearly flag practical limitations (computational inefficiency of large filters, lack of code release) and the imperfect nature of the VGG-based evaluation metric.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a clear empirical contribution with correct implementation of Gram-based models and useful comparisons, but they also identify similar methodological weaknesses—heavy reliance on a VGG-based perceptual proxy, absence of human perceptual validation, limited statistical reporting, and lack of deeper theoretical explanation or key ablations. These issues limit confidence in the generality and robustness of the surprising claim that data-independent filters suffice, so a modest deduction is warranted despite solid experimental work. Consequently I assign a score of 3 to reflect promising empirical results but important gaps in evaluation and theory.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents clear empirical claims and provides experiments comparing single-layer random/multi-scale Gram models to VGG- and handcrafted baselines, but the work lacks principled theoretical analysis explaining why data-independent filters suffice — a key surprising claim for which I would expect identifiability or statistical arguments. The experimental methodology appears incomplete from the summary: evaluation relies heavily on a VGG-based loss known to be an imperfect perceptual proxy, there is no mention of multiple random seeds or statistical tests to establish robustness, and important ablations (e.g., learned vs random filters, filter count/nonlinearity isolated) are not convincingly reported. There are therefore logical gaps and limited statistical rigor that prevent full technical validation of the conclusions, though the empirical evidence does suggest the claims may hold in many cases.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and provides solid experimental evidence: single-layer random-filter Grammian models (including multi-scale variants) are compared against the VGG-based Gatys model and Portilla & Simoncelli on ImageNet textures, with reproducible optimization details reported. Mathematical components (Gram computation and optimization) are straightforward and appear correct. My main technical reservations are methodological: the evaluation relies heavily on a VGG-based perceptual loss (acknowledged by the authors as imperfect) and there is no reported human perceptual study to validate visual quality or diversity, which weakens broad claims about matching perceptual quality of deep supervised representations. Overall the conclusions are supported for the tested settings, but the absence of human evaluation and the limited discussion of variability vs. fidelity justify a minor deduction.

main_claims: A single-layer convolutional neural network with random, data-independent filters can synthesize natural textures that often rival state-of-the-art multi-layer VGG-based methods.

Depth and supervised pre-training of multi-layer representations are not necessary for high-quality parametric texture synthesis; multi-scale and/or many feature maps and nonlinearities are more critical.

A minimal baseline using Gram matrices of single-layer feature maps, optimized via L-BFGS-B, provides strong performance across many textures and can outperform older hand-crafted methods (e.g., Portilla & Simoncelli).
Combining filters at multiple spatial scales (multi-scale model) is important to capture small-, mid-, and long-range texture statistics; non-linearities (ReLU) are indispensable for good synthesis.
_summary.methodology: Compute Gram (covariance) matrices of feature activations from a single-layer CNN (various filter types and scales, including random filters) and synthesize textures by optimizing an input image from noise to match the reference Gram matrices using L-BFGS-B.
baselines: Gatys et al. (2015a) VGG-based texture model
metrics: VGG-based reconstruction cost (VGG-loss computed as average normalized Gram-matrix distances across selected VGG layers)
limitations: Multi-scale single-layer model is computationally inefficient due to large spatial filters (up to 55×55).
VGG-based loss is an imperfect proxy for human perceptual similarity and can fail for very regular man-made structures.
Balancing perceptual similarity and sample variability is challenging; the proposed random single-layer models tend to favor perceptual similarity at the cost of variability.
For finite-size images and many constraints, synthesis can lead to near-exact reconstruction of the reference (over-constrained / low variability).
_summary.topic_area: Computer Vision - texture synthesis / generative image models

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in machine learning with a research focus on theoretical understanding of deep and statistical models; extensive experience proving identifiability/generalization results for representation learning.
traits: Requires formal or at least principled theoretical explanation for surprising empirical claims (will downgrade if paper offers mainly empirical heuristics without analysis).
Penalizes papers that lack statistical rigor: expects quantitative comparisons with statistical tests, multiple random seeds, and explicit runtime/memory complexity analyses.

Demands ablation studies that isolate individual factors (e.g., effect of filter randomness vs learned filters, filter count, nonlinearity) with clear metrics; will deduct points if key ablations are missing or inconclusive.
Rarely impressed by incremental empirical baselines; expects conceptual novelty or explanatory insight beyond 'this works' demonstrations.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I evaluate whether the work provides principled understanding and reproducible, statistically-robust evidence for its core claims rather than presenting mainly empirical demonstrations.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Empiricist
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist in computer vision focused on generative models and applied image synthesis; extensive experience building and evaluating strong empirical baselines and reproducible benchmarks.
traits: Rewards simple, well-engineered baselines that challenge community assumptions (will upscore if a minimal method outperforms or matches complex prior work across many cases).

Values thorough empirical evaluation on diverse datasets and qualitative/quantitative comparisons to state-of-the-art; will penalize only if evaluation is visibly incomplete (e.g., tiny test set or missing important baselines).
Favors reproducibility: expects code release, detailed optimization/hyperparameter settings, and clear training/synthesis protocols; will increase score if provided.
Appreciates work that provides practical insights about architecture/design choices (e.g., importance of scale and nonlinearity) even without full theoretical explanation.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I focus on practical impact: clarity, strength of empirical evidence, robustness of baselines, and whether the work changes how practitioners should build systems.
